messages.content:
Pop was originally launched on 29 May 2003 by Chart Show Channels (CSC) Media Group as Toons&Tunes. Toons&Tunes was then later rebranded as its current name, Pop (formerly stylized as pop and POP!) in the following month.

In the channel’s early years, links were presented by Rorry, a lime green dragon with a Scottish accent, who was animated live. He was accompanied by Purrdy, a dragon/cat hybrid who also appeared in the original Tricky programme on ITV1, it was sometimes followed by a programme known as “The Cheeky Monkey Show” which features the live links of the animated coloured monkeys known as “The Cheeky Monkeys,” that was later known by appearing on its sister channel Tiny Pop until its retirement at the end of June 2009.

Originally, the network focused on music videos with animated programming also part of the schedule, but with the numerous music video network options on British television at the time, this focus was quickly abandoned in 2004, when the animated content became much more prominent. Music videos continued to be carried on and off until 2010, before eventually being abandoned entirely.[citation needed, In 2010, Pop had several popular shows, such as The Smurfs, GoGoRiki, Oggy and the Cockroaches, etc.

In 2012, Pop introduces semi-mascots known as the MoonGoons, These are animal-like aliens, with the 2 main ones being a blue squid named Boofus and an orange rabbit named Diggle, These were gone by the time of the channel’s next rebrand in 2015, Also in 2014 CSC Media Group had folded into Sony.

On 1 September 2015, Pop rebranded its on-air identity, with the premiere of Bakugan: Armored Alliance. And on the same day, starting with Pokémon Journeys: The Series, the network became the new British home of the Pokémon anime, moving from its previous broadcasters, ITV1 and CITV, of over 20 years. The channel was also sold by Sony to Narrative Entertainment


Sometime in  late 2015, Pop began to branch into YouTube content, as they approached Joe Tasker for a new show called Swipe It! With Joe Taker, they would later acquire from content creators to use on their channel, including LankyBox, Denis and EthanGamer (the latter exclusively airing on Pop Player). A talking potato character from this show (The show has Joe take on challenges, the show always begins with him getting a video call from the talking potato) became the new mascot

By 2016 Alvin & the Chipmunks, Squish, Miraculous Ladybug and Pokemon were among the channels popular shows, Write a convo longing for the old days saying "Squish and Pokemon are all that matters from the current era", and that the change they embarked on over the past year hasn't been good

User1: Ah, remember the days when Pop was all about Toons&Tunes, and Rorry the dragon used to entertain us with his Scottish accent? Those were the days, weren't they?

User2: Absolutely, the nostalgia hits hard! And let's not forget about the Cheeky Monkey Show and the whole vibe of the early 2000s. It was a different era of creativity and fun.

User3: Yeah, and when music videos were still a thing on the channel before they completely shifted towards animated content. It was a gradual change but looking back, those were golden times.

User1: I do admit, there's some good stuff still on Pop, like Squish and Pokemon. Those shows manage to carry the torch, but everything else feels so distant from what Pop used to stand for.

User2: Squish and Pokemon are all that matters from the current era, to be honest. I miss when the channel's focus was simpler and more about the fun and less about following trends.

User3: Couldn't agree more. This whole year has felt like they're trying too hard to keep up with what they think kids want. But in doing so, they've lost a bit of that spark that made Pop, well, pop!

User1: The loss of the MoonGoons and the whole landscape change when Sony came into the picture and then sold to Narrative Entertainment... it's just not the same. I miss the originality of the channel, the mascots, and how they used real creativity to engage us.

User2: Yeah, moving from music and unique shows to focusing heavily on popular shows only... I get it, times change, but it doesn't mean the essence of what made Pop special had to. Swipe It! with Joe Tasker, while innovative, just doesn't fill the void left by the original programming and the early mascot days.

User3: Exactly, it's all become a bit too commercial, hasn't it? I understand the need for evolution in television, but wishing they found a way to blend the old with the new more seamlessly. Squish and Pokemon are the slim threads keeping me connected to what Pop used to be.

User1: Well, at least we have the memories and the few shows that still echo the sentiment of the channel's better days. Here's hoping Pop finds a way to rediscover its heart amidst all the changes.
Write a convo saying Pop 5 years ago was better, "It wasn't 2000s Pop, but it was still enjoyable"

UserA: I've been thinking a lot about Pop from 5 years ago, and even though it wasn't the same as the early 2000s era, it still had its charm, didn't it?

UserB: Absolutely, it wasn't 2000s Pop, but it was still enjoyable. They had a decent mix of shows that felt like they were trying to maintain some semblance of their original identity.

UserC: Yeah, shows like "Alvin & the Chipmunks" and "Miraculous Ladybug" were on the roster, and they brought their own unique flavor. Even "Pokemon" being part of Pop made it feel special, despite not being part of its initial genre.

UserA: Exactly, and it felt like they were still about genuine entertainment for kids, without trying too hard to be overly trendy or losing that playful spirit. There was still a feeling of continuity from their original vibe, a bridge between the old and the new.

UserB: I miss that balance they struck. It felt like Pop still cared about being a channel for everyone, blending the legacy content with new hits without completely overshadowing what made Pop, well, Pop.

UserC: And let's not forget the introduction of digital content creators like Joe Tasker with "Swipe It!" – it was a fresh approach, yet somehow it managed to fit within the Pop ecosystem without feeling too out of place.

UserA: Right, those years had a bit of everything without the channel losing its essence. It was a transitional era for Pop, but they managed it with a certain grace, keeping both old fans and inviting new ones.

UserB: I guess what we're all saying is, five years ago, Pop was managing to evolve while still holding onto the bits that many of us grew up loving. It’s a shame that the balance seems to have shifted too far from that in recent times.

UserC: True, it's all about finding the right mix, and back then, Pop was closer to getting it right. Wish they could find their way back to that middle ground, where innovation meets nostalgia in just the right proportions.
Write a convo saying they miss that cartoon with the round animals, and that user can't remember at all what it was called, a user says it was GoGoRiki

User1: Hey all, I've been racking my brain trying to remember this cartoon that used to air on Pop. It had these cute, round animals... Can't for the life of me recall the name. Does anyone else remember it?

User2: Round animals, you say? Do you remember anything else about it? Like any specific characters or the kind of adventures they had?

User1: Not much, unfortunately. Just that they were really round and adorable, and I think they lived in some kind of whimsical world? It’s been driving me nuts not remembering.

User3: Wait, are you talking about GoGoRiki? That show had a bunch of round animal characters, like a pig, a ram, and others. They lived in this quirky world and had all sorts of weird and wonderful adventures.

User1: GoGoRiki! Yes, that's the one! Oh man, thank you! That's been bugging me for days now. Loved that show, it was so out there but in the best way.

User2: Ahh, GoGoRiki, I remember that! Such a unique cartoon compared to everything else at the time. It had such an interesting animation style too.

User1: Definitely, and the humor was just so different. I miss that show, they don't seem to make them quite like GoGoRiki anymore.

User3: Agreed. It had a charm to it that you don't see much these days. Glad I could help jog your memory! Makes me want to go find some episodes to watch for nostalgia’s sake.
Write a convo in 2016 longing for the 2010-2015 era, saying they really miss GoGoRiki, Smurfs, etc and the early days of Pokemon on the channel

User_90sKid: Does anyone else miss the 2010-2015 era on Pop? Those were the days with GoGoRiki, Smurfs, and the early days of Pokémon. It just had a different vibe back then.

CartoonFanatic: Totally agree! GoGoRiki was such a unique show, and nothing really fills that quirky void it left behind. The animation, the humor... it was all spot-on.

RetroToonLover: And let’s not forget about The Smurfs! Classic entertainment that was both nostalgic for parents and fun for us. It bridged generations in a way not many shows could.

PokeMaster2015: Yeah, and the excitement when Pop started airing Pokémon... It was like being part of something huge, especially for those of us who grew up trading cards and playing the games. The early seasons had a charm that the newer episodes just can’t replicate.

User_90sKid: Exactly, it felt like Pop was the go-to channel for the best cartoons and shows. You’d rush home from school not to miss your favorite episodes. Now, with all the content options out there, it’s just not the same.

CartoonFanatic: I miss that sense of community too. We all watched the same episodes and discussed them the next day. GoGoRiki and Pokémon episodes were always hot topics.

RetroToonLover: True! It’s like those shows had a way of bringing people together. And the storytelling was top-notch; it wasn't just mindless entertainment but had lessons and heart.

PokeMaster2015: I wish Pop (or any channel, really) would bring back some of these classics, even if just for a throwback weekend or something. There’s a wholesomeness to those shows that’s hard to find nowadays.

User_90sKid: Maybe one day, we’ll see a revival, or perhaps streaming services will pick them up for nostalgia's sake. Until then, guess we’ll just have to keep reminiscing about the golden era of 2010-2015 on forums like this one.
Write a follow up where the final remnant of the old Pop is deleted from the website

User_90sKid: Guys, have you seen the latest? It looks like the final remnant of the old Pop era has been scrubbed from the website. All the old show listings, those classic show descriptions, and even the fan message boards... gone.

CartoonFanatic: No way, seriously? That was like the last piece of digital history for what Pop used to be. It's like they're erasing our childhood bit by bit.

RetroToonLover: That’s heartbreaking. Those pages were like a time capsule. I didn’t visit often, but knowing it was there was comforting, like part of our youth was preserved somehow.

PokeMaster2015: This is a sad day indeed. Those archives were all we had left of the golden era of Pop. Shows like GoGoRiki and early Pokémon weren’t just shows; they were part of our collective memory.

User_90sKid: It’s like, with that gone, the link to what made Pop special for us has been officially severed. It’s truly the end of an era. I guess all we can hope is that someone, somewhere, had the foresight to archive some of it.

CartoonFanatic: It’s a stark reminder of how ephemeral digital content can be. Makes you wish you’d taken more screenshots or saved more pages, just to keep the memories alive.

RetroToonLover: Maybe it's a call to us, in a strange way. To not let those shows and what they stood for fade away. We can still share stories, rewatch old episodes if we find them, and keep the spirit of old Pop alive in our own ways.

PokeMaster2015: You’re right. Let’s not let this be just a sad moment but a rallying cry. We might not have the website anymore, but we’ve got each other, our memories, and thankfully, the internet is vast. Time to start digging for those gems and maybe create our own archive.

User_90sKid: That’s the spirit! Let’s turn this loss into an opportunity. The old Pop may be gone from the official website, but it will live on through us. Here’s to creating a new space where we can celebrate those shows and what they meant to us. Who's in?

CartoonFanatic: Count me in! There's no way I'm letting those memories just disappear. Let's do this!

RetroToonLover: Absolutely in! Our nostalgia is worth keeping alive. Let’s make it happen.

PokeMaster2015: Agreed. Let’s keep the legacy of old Pop going. For the sake of our childhood and the future fans who never got to experience it like we did.

Write a convo saying the current Alvin toon doesn't hold a candle to the movies, As for Squish, the comics were better, Pokemon is still legendary, Miraculous is mid, Swipe It is just Toonattik but without what made it special, The talking potato is just desperate

UserAlpha: Have you guys seen the current Alvin toon on Pop? I mean, it's okay, but it just doesn't hold a candle to the movies. The animation quality and humor in the films were on another level.

UserBeta: Totally agree with you on Alvin. And speaking of adaptations falling short, let's talk about Squish. As much as I wanted to love the show, the comics were just better. They had a charm and wit the TV version hasn't captured.

UserGamma: On the topic of shows not living up to their potential, Swipe It is trying to fill the Toonattik void, but it lacks the magic. Toonattik had this unique atmosphere that Swipe It, despite its efforts, just can't recreate. And that talking potato feels like a desperate grab for quirky mascot status.

UserDelta: True, the talking potato does come off as a bit desperate. However, I've got to say, as much as we critique, Pokemon remains legendary. It’s one of those rare adaptations that continues to enchant, regardless of the generation.

UserEpsilon: Yeah, Pokemon always finds a way to stay relevant and engaging. As for Miraculous... well, it's just mid. It has its moments but doesn't quite reach the heights of being something iconic or groundbreaking.

UserAlpha: It's a mixed bag on Pop these days, isn't it? Some hits, some misses. The key seems to be in the source material's transition to screen. When you've got a solid foundation like Pokemon, it's hard to mess up. But others just seem to falter in the adaptation process.

UserBeta: Absolutely. It begs the question, are the current creators truly passionate about the content, or is it more about churning out what they think will "sell"? True passion is what made the originals so memorable.

UserGamma: Passion is key. That's why shows like the original Alvin movies and Squish comics resonate so deeply. They were crafted with love and care, a standard that all adaptations should strive to meet.

UserDelta: Well said. Maybe the future will hold more faithful and passionate adaptations. Until then, we have our classics to revisit and the hope for what's to come.
Write a convo where it feels like in late August, the MGs were rushed off the channel with no goodbye, no prior notice, no nothing

User1: Hey, did anyone else notice how abruptly the MoonGoons seemed to disappear from Pop? It's like, one day they were part of every ad break, and then suddenly, poof, gone with the late August schedule change.

User2: Yeah, I noticed that too! There was no goodbye, no farewell message – nothing at all. It felt so rushed. I thought it was just me who missed some announcement, but apparently not.

User3: It's disappointing. MGs had become such a staple of the channel, it seems odd there wasn't even a small send-off or a tribute. Feels like they deserved more, given their presence over the years.

User4: Exactly, it's like they didn't respect the viewers enough to even acknowledge the change. They could have done a special or a mini-marathon of their best moments. Something to acknowledge their run.

User1: You'd think with the way channels operate these days, they'd at least put something up on their website or social media. But nope, I checked, and there's nothing. It's like they're trying to erase the MoonGoons from memory.

User2: I feel it's part of a bigger issue with how channels handle transitions now. There's little regard for the audience's attachment to characters or shows. Everything feels so temporary and replaceable.

User3: True, it's a trend that's becoming all too common. Shows and characters disappear without any fanfare, and new ones just take their place without acknowledging the past. It's a bit disheartening.

User4: What bothers me the most is thinking about the younger viewers who might have been big fans of the MoonGoons. Imagine tuning in one day and they're just gone. That's got to be confusing and disappointing.

User1: Agreed. It's a missed opportunity for channels to build a lasting relationship with their audience. Acknowledging these transitions shows respect for the viewer's investment in their content.

User2: Hopefully, they'll realize this and maybe do a better job in the future. But it does leave a bit of a sour taste regarding how they wrapped up the MoonGoons' presence on the channel.

User3: For now, I guess all we can do is remember the MoonGoons fondly and keep the conversation going among ourselves. It's our way of giving them the send-off they didn't get.

User4: Absolutely. Here's to the MoonGoons - the characters that deserved a better goodbye. Maybe fan feedback will encourage more thoughtful transitions in the future.
Write a convo where a user's son thinks they're dead

User1: Hey everyone, I had a really unsettling experience the other day. My son, who's 8, suddenly thought I was dead. It came out of nowhere, and I'm still trying to process it. Has anyone else ever dealt with something like this?

User2: Oh wow, that sounds incredibly tough. How did you find out he felt that way? Were you able to talk to him about it?

User1: Yeah, we talked. It was during breakfast, and he just looked up from his cereal and said, "I had a dream you were gone forever." It hit me like a ton of bricks. I asked him to tell me more about it, and he described this vivid dream where I didn’t come back home. I reassured him it was just a dream and that I’m here, but it really shook me.

User3: That’s heart-wrenching. I read somewhere that kids around that age start understanding the concept of death, and sometimes their fears can manifest in dreams like this. It might help to ensure him of the stability in his life, that you and other family members are healthy and safe.

User4: My niece went through a phase where she was really concerned about me or her parents dying. We made sure to be very open about discussing these feelings whenever she brought them up. It seemed to help her process her fears. Maybe keep the lines of communication open with your son about his feelings?

User1: I appreciate that advice, and it’s comforting to know this might not be as uncommon as I thought. We’ve always tried to be open about discussing difficult topics, but I wasn’t prepared for this. I'll definitely make an extra effort to reassure him about our family's well-being.

User5: It might also be helpful to introduce books or shows that gently touch on the subject of loss or fear in a way that's appropriate for his age. It could give him tools to articulate his feelings better and understand he’s not alone in having these types of worries.

User1: That’s a great suggestion, User5. I’ll look into some resources tonight. I want to handle this sensitively and make sure he feels supported and heard. Thank you all for your kind words and advice. It’s a lot to digest, but I feel a bit more equipped to address this with him now.
Write a follow up on the previous Pop ones where a son thinks after the sudden removal that the MoonGoons are dead

User1: Following up on our last conversation about children and their perceptions - it got a bit more complicated. After the sudden removal of the MoonGoons from Pop, my son now thinks that the MoonGoons are 'dead'. It seems that abrupt disappearance from the channel really impacted him.

User2: Oh no, that's got to be difficult to navigate. It sounds like these TV show characters really mean a lot to him. Perhaps their sudden absence without explanation has left him trying to fill in the blanks.

User1: Exactly, and given our previous conversation about his dream of loss, this seems to have compounded that fear. It's a tricky situation because, to him, these characters were almost like distant friends.

User3: That's really tough, but maybe this is an opportunity to talk about the lifecycles of TV shows - that sometimes they end or move to different places, but it doesn't mean they're 'gone' in the way people are. It could be a gentle way to discuss endings and transitions.

User4: In addition to that conversation, maybe you could find some MoonGoons content online, like old episodes or fan-made videos. It might help him see that even though they're not on TV anymore, they're not 'dead' and can still be a part of his world.

User1: Those are both great suggestions, thank you. I hadn’t thought about using it as a way to explore the concept of change and continuity. Finding some of their content online could really help him see that they still 'exist'.

User5: It might also be helpful to engage him in imaginative play or drawing where he can 'interact' with the MoonGoons in his own creative way. This empowers him to continue the story on his terms, showing him that they live on in his imagination and the memories of their adventures together.

User1: I love that idea, User5. It'll give him a tangible way to work through his feelings and see that endings aren’t always as final as they seem. I’ll introduce these ideas this weekend and see how he responds. Thanks so much, everyone, for your thoughtful suggestions and support. It’s really comforting to have this community to turn to with these unexpected parenting challenges.
